    CAGEMATCH » Promotions Database » World Wrestling Entertainment (WWE)

    World Wrestling Entertainment (WWE)

    Active promotion in the North America region

    General Data
    Current name:
    World Wrestling Entertainment
    Current abbreviation:
    WWE
    Status:
    Active
    Location:
    Stamford, Connecticut, USA
    Active Time:
    1948 - today
    WWW:
     https://www.wwe.com
     https://www.facebook.com/WWE
     https://x.com/WWE
     https://www.instagram.com/wwe
     https://www.tiktok.com/@wwe
     https://www.threads.net/@wwe

    Details
    Names:
    World Wrestling Entertainment (05.05.2002 - today)
    World Wrestling Federation (30.03.1979 - 04.05.2002)
    World Wide Wrestling Federation (24.01.1963 - 29.03.1979)
    National Wrestling Alliance Capitol Wrestling Federation (1952 - 23.01.1963)
    National Wrestling Alliance Capital Sports (1948 - 1952)
    Abbreviations:
    WWE, WWF, WWWF, NWA Capitol Wrestling, NWA Capital Sports
    Owners:
    TKO Group Holdings (2023 - today)
    Vince McMahon (1982 - 2023)
    Vince McMahon Sr. (1952 - 1982)
    Jess McMahon & Toots Mondt (1948 - 1952)
    Popular events:
    WrestleMania, SummerSlam, Royal Rumble, Survivor Series, NXT TakeOver/NXT UK TakeOver, King Of The Ring, Elimination Chamber, Hell In A Cell, Money In The Bank, Extreme Rules, Backlash, Judgment Day, Unforgiven, No Mercy, Fastlane, Battleground, Payback, Night Of Champions/Clash Of Champions, Armageddon, In Your House, TLC, No Way Out
    TV & Online:
    Monday Night RAW/RAW is WAR/RAW, SmackDown/Thursday Night SmackDown/Friday Night SmackDown/SmackDown Live, NXT/NXT 2.0, NXT UK, NXT Level Up, 205 Live, Main Event, Superstars, Tough Enough, ECW on Sci Fi/ECW on Syfy, Heat/Sunday Night Heat, Velocity, Jakked, Prime Time Wrestling, Saturday Night's Main Event, Super Astros, Challenge, All American Wrestling, Championship Wrestling, All-Star Wrestling/All Star Wrestling, MSG Network, Mania, Action Zone, Saturday Morning Slam, Shotgun Saturday Night, Speed, LFG, EVOLVE
    Logos:

    WWE World Tag Team ChampionshipThe Vision (Austin Theory & Bron Breakker) (2)
    Introduction
    This text is hidden because it is only available in German language. Please click this line if you do not care and want to view it anyway.
    World Wrestling Entertainment ist die größte und bekannteste Wrestlingliga aller Zeiten. Unter der Führung von Vince McMahon begann in den 80er Jahren das Zeitalter einer neuen Form des Wrestlings, bei der die Charaktere und die Geschichten abseits des Rings eine genauso hohe und teilweise sogar höhere Priorität bekamen, als die tatsächlichen Kämpfe. Aus Wrestling wurde "Sports Entertainment" und Wrestler wurden zu "Superstars". Mit der Kreation von "WrestleMania", der Zugkraft von Stars wie Hulk Hogan, Randy Savage und später The Ultimate Warrior stieg McMahon schnell auf den Thron der Wrestlingpromoter, nur um im Zuge seines Erfolges viele kleinere Territorien und deren Stars aufzukaufen und damit das Territorialzeitalter der Wrestlinggeschichte effektiv zu beenden. Bis in die 90er Jahre war McMahons Liga, damals noch unter dem Namen "World Wrestling Federation", die unangefochtene Nummer 1 in den USA, die Stars wie Bret Hart, den Undertaker oder Shawn Michaels hervorbrachte.

    Als in den 90er Jahren mit World Championship Wrestling eine ernsthafte Konkurrenz auf sich aufmerksam und der WWF diverse Stars - allen voran Hogan - abspenstig machte, veränderte McMahon nach einer Phase der finanziellen Erfolglosigkeit mit der kreativen Mithilfe von Vince Russo die Mainstream-Wrestlinglandschaft erneut, als er sich selbst als Oberbösewicht in eine aktive Rolle vor die Kameras begab, um dem exponentiell an Popularität gewinnenden Steve Austin gegenüberzutreten. Diese Zeit Ende der 90er ging als "Attitude-Ära" in die Geschichte des Wrestlings ein, in der nicht nur neue Stars wie The Rock, Mick Foley oder Triple H geboren, sondern auch das meiste Geld eingenommen und die höchsten Einschaltquoten erreicht wurden. Die WWF wurde so populär und finanziell erfolgreich, dass sie sogar den Gang an die Börse wagte und Anfang 2001 den zu Boden gerungenen Konkurrenten WCW aufkaufen konnte.

    Das Einverleiben der rivalisierenden Promotion zementierte die Position als Marktführer, Einschaltquoten und Pay-Per-View-Buyrates sanken nach dem Ende des Monday Night Wars jedoch ab und erreichten bis heute nie mehr das Niveau der Attitude-Ära - die Karriere-Enden von Austin und Rock trug zu der Entwicklung bei. Auch die Einführung der Brand Extension, einer Aufteilung des Rosters in zwei (ab 2006: drei) einzelne sogenannte "Brands" als eine Art Ersatz für den Konkurrenzkampf mit anderen Promotions schaffte es nicht, die glorreichen Tage wieder aufleben zu lassen.

    Ab etwa 2005 prägten neue Stars wie Batista, Randy Orton, der in der WWE extrem popularisierte Rey Mysterio und vor allem John Cena, der erfolgreichste Star nach der Attitude-Ära, das Bild. Die letzteren beiden stehen seit 2008 auch im Zentrum einer Neuausrichtung der Liga, die in die genau andere Richtung als die Attitude-Ära ging: Die PG-Ära, in der die WWE ihre Inhalte und Charaktere kindgerecht zu gestalten versucht, um dem zunehmenden Anteil an jüngeren Fans Rechnung zu tragen.

    Trotz des Abschwungs nach der Attitude-Ära ist die WWE weiterhin ein Unternehmen, das schwarze Zahlen schreibt und mit Hilfe von drei TV-Sendungen pro Woche ("Monday Night RAW", "Superstars" und "SmackDown!") sowie mindestens monatlichen Pay Per Views und wöchentlichen House Shows die größte Zuschauerbandbreite der Welt anspricht. Auch in Deutschland ist die WWE die erste (und oftmals auch die einzige) Liga, die Wrestlingfans oder Nichtfans mit Wrestling assoziieren, was sich auf langfristige Sicht wahrscheinlich auch nicht ändern wird.
    7.6​9
    Current Total Rating (?)

    Other:
    • Details & History
    • Ratings System Rules
    • Help and formula
    Latest comments of the CAGEMATCH inmates
    moonwater805 wrote on 10.07.2026:
    [1.0] "A once great promotion that has killed itself from the inside due to poor booking, senseless releases, backstage scandal after scandal, non-stop celebrity crap, and the TKO takeover focusing on profit and ad placement. They either need to clean house of all the Vince defenders (Brock, HHH, etc) or they will continue their fall from grace and end up the next WCW. They force talent to change their ring names so that they own the rights, don't let them work indy shows with talent from other promotions, and refuse to develop new stars. Shows like RAW are upwards of 3 hours long and barely feature an hour of in ring action. Plain and simple, WWE is a sports entertainment for profit company, not a pro wrestling promotion. HHH is a producer, not a promoter."
    saywhaaaadahelly wrote on 10.07.2026:
    [0.0] "TKO dropped the ball on WWE since they only bought the company just for the sake for owning money, and even with their debts gone, they'll still own the promotion just so that they could gain more money, hence why TKO/WWE is the literal definition of the seven deadly sins. And it's damn shame too because most of their wrestlers can actually wrestle while some others like Jey Uso, Liv Morgan, Nia Jax, Raquel Rodriguez, Chelsea Green, Rhea Ripley, Cody Rhodes, CM Punk and Roman Reigns are the only wrestlers who are either been seen getting a push or winning the belts that they shouldn't have won, though some can wrestle, it was very oversaturated for the same people like Rhodes, Ripley, Morgan, Punk, and Reigns to hold the main event belt. The ring works for the wrestlers are really really slow to th epoint even turtles can walk faster than them all because they want the fans to notice everything even though fans only care about the wrestler's characters and not the wrestling (which is understandable but you have to mention that wrestling is literally the #1 priority). Not to mention how shitty booking/creative is- holy shit, they act like they're WCW. Ripley is currently injured but her belt won't be vacated because she is so god damn protected, Dominik when he was the IC champ didn't vacate it as well whilst being injured, and the booking of the women's champs holding women's belt like Stephanie (who is getting repeatedly booked against either Nikki or Raquel.), IYO (they stopped booking her the last two months of her reign), Tiffany Stratton (she lost momentum), Giulia (they just kept on repeating of not booking her whenever he popularity is starting to gain, which is making her look bad and to the wwe fans make her seem like shes a weak one.) and the ironic vignettes of wrestlers not debuting until further notice which is what blake monroe/mariah may had been suffering from. Also the theme songs for wrestlers are shitty man, just actually pay people and also THEY USE FUCKING AI FOR THEIR GRAPHIC POSTERS AND VIGNETTES AND SCRIPTED PROMOS AS WELL LIKE HOW CAN YOU BE A BILLIONAIRE COMPANY TO JUST SO YOU CAN CUT CORNERS ALL BECAUSE YOU DON'T WANNA HIRE A FUCKING GRAPHIC DESIGNER/VIDEO DESIGNER AND ACTUALLY PAID THEM TO DO THE WORK FOR YOU?"
    jhuanherbas wrote on 09.07.2026:
    [5.0] "A company that has made more bad than good to pro wrestling. Too many highs and lows, inconsistency and disgusting moments. Being commanded by a sex offender for a long time certainly helped. Difficult being a fan, and the Triple H and TKO era changed nothing."
    Blacklotus wrote on 03.07.2026:
    [0.0] "Extremely disappointed and do regret the day I discovered wrestling all together due to the fact how supposedly Vince McMahon sold and left the company and I thought it would get better but that was way too good to be true. Where do I start, the system created by VKM is still there to this day which is why it feels the same, 1st thing they do that pisses me off is they're still practicing that lame phony looking "Wwe style" a style of professional wrestling that doesn't make sense and makes the in-ring action look faker that it is and unfortunately whenever they sign a hot free agent or a popular indy/international wrestlers they always have to change their names and gimmicks and sure I get it! Like copy rights BS or whatever but Goddamn it why they have to force them to assimilate to the Wwe-style which almost always waters them down where they look as talented as Kelly Kelly. Another problem is their ring never makes a noise or an impact especially when a wwe superstar does sit-outs or drivers moves like Lyra or Guilia, like remember back in good years of TNA before Hogan butchered it they had a joshi named Hamada who did an awesome Michinoko Driver where you heard and felt the impact of the move where when Lyra does her Night Wing finish you don't hear it making it look weak, sadly the solution from the Wwe fans and backstage officials is always "oh do a different finisher" And finally the camera cuts are awful I hate whenever they zoom in as the wrestler drops the opponent for the finish...."
    lordbosco1 wrote on 01.07.2026:
    "The main roster feels overloaded with shopworn, middle-aged wrestlers who are phoning in the same schtick they've been doing for years on end. It's past time the new generation takes the spotlight. Nostalgia is not good enough. Thus far, I prefer NXT where the wrestlers are young, hungry and eager to make new fans. They may still be works in progress, but they try harder, take bigger risks, and get better booking in NXT. I went to Wrestlemania 1. It was a bad card. As an old-schooler checking out the new scene: I like how wrestling has absorbed elements of MMA, reality TV, and Lucha Libre. Everybody be jumping off the top rope, though, and it just isn't special anymore. I get excited now when I see a chin lock. Rest holds can be made into crowd moments, I have seen it with mine own eyes. Some of the new DDT and cutter moves don't look convincing to me. I never liked the swinging neck-breaker, either. Commercials during matches are an abomination. Live matches always had better pacing. I saw them at the LA Sports Arena. Got to see Brett Hart before he was the Hitman in a tag team with David Sammartino. Best live match: Ricky Steamboat vs. Randy Savage."
    tlaustin wrote on 28.06.2026:
    [3.0] "I no longer have a need for WWE anymore the same way I no longer have a need for a landline telephone. I recognize its invention and history, can point to moments where it has been useful and even entertaining for me in my life, and can only look at it as an archaic thing that needs to be done away with. Far more committed people have and will write far more interesting obituaries about this place than I can or will, and that's okay--I'd rather watch wrestling I enjoy elsewhere instead. It certainly isn't "adequate" anymore so 3 it is."
    notroubleluc wrote on 22.06.2026:
    [4.0] "I often feel like I'm not the audience for WWE, and it often feels like WWE tries to cater to the demographics of people over the age of 50 who lived and breathed the attitude era or kids under 13 with no attention span; so getting into modern WWE as a new viewer means you are hit with some very upfront presentation, and are constantly being told about prestige you've never actually seen. WWE does a very deft job handling this. Their presentation is grand, showy, and they do a lot to cater to all ages and casual viewers. Their place as *the* wrestling brand allows them incredible consistency in some areas, such as constantly pushing top talent in big stories that feels larger than life, allowing their women's division to maintain top support, and having a constant rotation of wrestlers being moved from it's developmental brands to keep things fresh. Unfortunately, WWE's constant dedication to showsmanship is very much its undoing, because they believe showsmanship and wrestling are seperate. Coming from someone with a athletic background and who admires the athletics of pro wrestling, WWE's shows can be too long with very little physical action and every single quirk of live entertainment that paints WWE as a unique sport, but with also the infinite greed a sport can embody: shows are bloated or just unnecessary to the overall watching experience, advertisements and ads are baked into the brand, constant over-the-top commentary, and stories that are designed for engagement but very little thought are just some of it's structural problems. WWE's culture outside the ring is also unfortunately incredibly jarring for someone who wants to engage the hobby of watching pro wrestling in a healthy manner; From no small amount of controversy that can range from management to the wrestlers themselves, and WWE's 'showsmanship' translating to cold hard captialism outside the shows affecting pro wrestling as a whole. At the end of the day, whatever positive and negatives there are, there are some extremely harsh negatives that stand out. It can be outright boring, it is a product that rewards you LESS the more you become invested into it, and it's general culture and fandom can be extremely off-putting. Great for watercooler talk. Anything past that requires a lot of compromise, and the best thing WWE has done is make me realize no amount of engagement with the hobby is worth that compromise."
    davbos wrote on 21.06.2026:
    [3.0] "lamentablemente cada dia es peor, considerando que nunca se han destacado por ser los mejores en lucha libre, si no que su lema va ligado a las historias y los feudos que desarrollan, existe una evidente falencia creativa e ilógica narrativa en sus shows... En la actualidad tienen a grandes luchadores, tal vez cambiar su enfoque en lucha y no tanto en intentar crear historias interesantes (que no lo logran) seria algo que ayudaría a mejorar su producto."
    tricklow wrote on 21.06.2026:
    "Calidad de mal en peor, cada show semanal, incluso cada PPV se siente como una perdida de tiempo... Se transformaron en una empresa sin alma"
    Personano wrote on 17.06.2026:
    [0.0] "WWE are the absolute antithesis of what makes a good wrestling company. Not only is their in-ring action dulled, dire, slow and uninspired, the company itself has frequently been involved in heinous scandals including full on sexual abuse, locker room harassment, actual racism and deals with the cruel and ruthless Saudi regime. If I had to definitively name a 'worst' major wrestling company as of 2026, it would be WWE. Even in their supposed heyday the product was still full of horrible garbage. Remember the demeaning 'Divas' era? I certainly do. And I feel a lot of others unfortunately don't. WWE will always be my least favourite wrestling company and with how their TKO deal has been going I doubt that's going to change."
    Killerhustle2008 wrote on 15.06.2026:
    [8.0] "I don't exactly hate WWE right now but honestly it's just boring. But overall I think WWE is a good company without the bullcrap from TKO. Or without Vince McMahon like I seriously can't believe that people want him back. I also think they should start focusing on younger stars like Bron Breakker, Oba Femi, and Trick Williams."
    Open The Vault wrote on 05.06.2026:
    [5.0] "[5.7] WWE's 2026 so far has been a fall from grace. 2025 we saw the budding issues accompany the WWE and hoped for a bit of a resurrection from a weak year but it has been horrid. It's gotten to 2019 levels of horrible. TKO has hurt WWE in ways that are unimaginable with their agenda of focusing on moments and celebrities and profits. 6 months in and we've seen the product get worse month by month and I'm excepting this to be the case for the rest of the year. The casuals are moving away slowly and it's sad to see. RAW has been objectively the same booked show every week since 2025, NXT has just been okay and nothing standoutish and SmackDown has been one of the worst pro-wrestling shows in the modern era (including NWA and JCW) Overall, WWE has been a downward trajectory and I don't see a recovery anytime soon."
    Cast wrote on 03.06.2026:
    [2.0] "Creo que en la actualidad esta pasando por un mal periodo la empresa... No se puede negar que ha tenido épocas brillantes donde se disfrutaba tanto de historias como de grandes luchadores.. Hoy lamentablemente hay baja calidad en las historias y a pesar de tener grandes talentos en el ring, con muy buenas habilidades, no logran entregar grandes combates, la preferencia por las figuras corporativas más que por el talento nato han estado afectando al producto"
    AtticusFinch wrote on 01.06.2026:
    [4.0] "10 years ago I had given WWE a 7 because it really felt like they were on an upswing at the time as the renewed interest in their brand split had made it feel pretty fresh, mainly on the SmackDown side of things though. I had my gripes that they were pretty stubborn still with booking and not giving into what the crowd thought was hot at the time. And here we are again. New boss same as the old boss. WWE has always been very corporate wrestling, which was fine, but the last year has really become inexcusable. Had I written this review in 2024 or early 2025, I might've gone as far to bump up my rating to an 8, maybe a 9 if I really felt generous at the time. It's become so profit driven and focusing on engagements rather than putting on compelling stories. To be fair to them, you'll still find some genuine gems and as of writing, they've got a batch of really promising talent. The only problem is they aren't doing anything with them. Back in the 2010s, people griped about part-timers reigning supreme and holding belts. Now you've got the main talent, faces of the company, holding belts hostage, rarely wrestling on TV if at all or not even around for weeks at a time. Even the weekly champions only hold the belts as props. The main event scene as of today is the same as it was 2 years ago. They build up these new, fresh faces just for an established vet to beat them and then push them back down. And I'm not oblivious to the fact that wrestling has always been secondary in WWE, but it really has only gotten worse in favor of shoehorning as many ads as humanly possible or an overbooked nightmare. Again, still some great matches to be found, but it isn't rewarded. A company with such a rich history, but these great years get amplified and revered so much because there are a lot of bad times and those years are all you can hold onto."
    SuperCherryBomb wrote on 31.05.2026:
    [6.0] "The overall story of the World Wrestling Federation/Entertainment is one of being reactive rather than proactive. Rarely if ever has the company ever revolutionized or put forth wholly original concepts. Such has even been admitted by various backstage hands and producers over the many decades of it's existence. At best, the WWF/E has taken what others have done and subsumed and molded such to the brand's ideals on sports entertainment and their presentation of it. Usually, this results in a watered down presentation of what it's taken inspiration (or just wholesale taking) from at worst, and at best is just transferring it over with no real significant change. There's very rarely any innovation to be found from most of the WWF/E's history in my opinion and because of that the entire thing can be looked at as a sort of malaise once you're like me and start really branching yourself out of it's mainstream stranglehold bubble. Even though the eras of the WWF/E certainly feel different in their own right from the Golden Era, to the New Generation, to the Attitude Era, Ruthless Aggression, PG, etc;. However, what the WWF/E has innovated in is one main aspect. Presentation. The company was built practically with the intent to be mainstream, to become a "show" based around wrestling and inject itself into the collective pop culture of America, and later the world, and to create the most digestible form of pro wrestling their can be (weird convictions and whims of Vince McMahon notwithstanding). It's because of this that so much of the WWF/E, while not innovative, has been inspiring for so many at both the lowest and highest levels of the profession at one time or another. Not to mention pertaining to different styles and eras associated with there in. The pomp and mythic statuses of the Golden Era where everyone was a Saturday Morning cartoon superhero or supervillain, the New Generation's silliness and goofy gimmicks that emphasized a strange but fun atmosphere that could underline a brutal sport that shone through everyone once in a while, the Attitude Era's rebelliousness and penchant for all black ring wear (or just jeans) and hardcore trashiness, the Ruthless Aggression work rate and ability to mix new and older school styles, the PG era's limitations from it's rating change requiring an entire switch in thought on what is needed from a match to entertain, and the newer Reality and New eras which had a focus on connecting with audiences outside of wrestling and also a focus on the ideals of long term storytelling above all else. The execution of each era's ideals was never all encompassing or at all successful in following such every time, but they were in focus enough and by the nature of being the number one dominating company for so long each era would come to define an era of a certain type of fan of wrestling which continually changed and evolved, and in turn which the WWF/E has adapted to. I don't think I could ever rate the WWF/E that high. It's sheer size, corporate bureaucracy in the way of creative, sleazy capitalistic tendencies over the art of wrestling (Vince famously saying he wishes he wasn't booking wrestling but just a general show, and the current new TKO owners not really inspiring confidence that they cared much any more than Vince), and it's strict adherence to a formula of wrestling that both largely didn't care about the actual wrestling and also actively stifles it at every turn doesn't allow for it to be rated highly to me. If anything any amazing things to come from WWF/E frequently feels like a happy accident than anything truly born with an intent to inspire. But again, by the nature of it's sheer size and longevity; and by nature of it's intended digestibility, it still does have many matches, moments, stories, and characters that have entertained and inspired not just me but many others. I'm not totally negative on it as a promotion, even if at the end of the day I think it has been a net negative on the industry of wrestling on the whole within its history."
    TheDoomCJ wrote on 29.05.2026:
    [1.0] "WWE feels more like a giant corporate brand than a wrestling company. Shows are packed with ads and sponsorships, the Saudi Arabia deals are pure cash grabs, and celebrities get more attention than actual wrestlers. The wrestling itself is slow, over-scripted, and repetitive, and above all else boring. Loads of talented wrestlers are wasted or barely used properly. The allegations around Vince McMahon disgust me. Brock Lesnar is still being pushed despite being linked to abhorrent behaviour. WWE sold its soul for money and corporate image. If for some reason anyone is reading this, please watch any of Stardom, AEW, CMLL, AJPW, NJPW, TJPW, Mystery Wrestling, Rev Pro, any of your local indies. WWE is pro wrestling for Disney adults - Support REAL wrestling."
    GoogleHomeUce wrote on 27.05.2026:
    "WWE, the greatest professional wrestling company of all time, people won't agree with that but it is, no doubt about it, just gotta be straightforward and honest, they are, 100%."
    Akarai7 wrote on 26.05.2026:
    [1.0] "The wrestling in this company is dead, it's just ads, celebrities, sponsors, the matches are slow, i miss WWE from 20 years ago. When you love pro wrestling it's almost unwatchable. Thank you NXT Black & Gold you carried this company for years, and now even if it's not what it used to be NXT still is carrying the company. Honestly, the main roster has just became TKO World Entertainment with some wrestling in it but not much. If you really love pro wrestling, move along, there are other companies that currently offer pro wrestling, but that's no longer the case with WWE. The years 2000 are very loooong gone."
    Mister Cute Face wrote on 25.05.2026:
    [2.0] "I can no longer objectively rate this company from a wrestling perspective. This rating reflects the entertainment entity that the WWE has become. This is a company that shows time and again that they will do everything they can to monopolize the industry and culturally sanitize and neuter everything that is not in the universe. TKO will be taking up space on 4 of the biggest network and streaming platforms in 2026. And now we are talking about in match ads at PLEs? Trying to get in with the NFL/ESPN/UFC set is silly. Stephen A. Smith will not move any needle for WWE. When WWE works, it's because it exists in the fantastical world of wrestling. Sports fans don't watch UFC or the NFL for characters or worked matches, they tune in because it's a competition. They fundamentally exist in completely different realms than wrestling. TKO is too big. This is not good for entertainment, wrestling, streaming, nor the consumer. I was a huge fan because the network made everything accessible for a low cost. The black and gold era of NXT was my favorite modern era for wrestling. Ciampa vs Gargano was my favorite modern feud for years. That magic is entirely dead. It's wild that the same guy in charge of black and gold is completely lost with the main roster. I watched WWE because it was fun, convenient and relatively cheap. It's now more than doubled the original price, requires multiple streaming services and has no magic. I don't need to be a part of it anymore."
    mattrozennyc wrote on 25.05.2026:
    [10.0] "The greatest wrestling promotion of all time. Yea, its had its ups and downs, but it has had many great moments over the years, and will forever be the best. The attitude-ruthless agression eras + 2022-2024 is some of the greatest pro wrestling you will ever watch, and even though there may be some low points, nothing can ever come close to WWE."
    Wwehul500 wrote on 20.05.2026:
    "I love wwe it's much different these days but sometimes it is for the better I like the product I watch it every week"
    PhoenixS99 wrote on 15.05.2026:
    [1.0] "It breaks my heart to do this but I cannot in sound mind give WWE any higher. If it wasn't for their rich history this would be a 0 as the recent Wrestlemania event was one of the saddest things I have ever seen in my life and that wasn't even their worst show of 2026. Constant forced celebrity involvement, commercials after literally every single match on PLEs (worst part out of all my issues) and dire storytelling, the company is sadly dead. They may be making money due to the TKO deal and Saudi Arabian blood money but that cannot last forever...I truly fear for the future of WWE because one more year of this product and they may be in serious trouble. Recent events like Rey Mysterio losing an eye, Zombies being used on the show, the disgusting releases of loyal workers like R Truth and the New Day show how painfully out of touch they have become, WWE need to wake up and they need to wake up right now!"
    daichi wrote on 11.05.2026:
    [10.0] "Bloodline storyline made me come back to wrestling 10 years later. Of course there are ups and downs but with events like wrestlemania 40, I am happy to look forward to those highs."
    Ben27 wrote on 10.05.2026:
    "All I can say about this company in 2026 is that they're bad at booking & they don't care. I have been watching since I was a little boy, and I have grown up with this company being the focal point of my television screen, and as a professional wrestling fan, I have come to terms with the fact that this company isn't a wrestling company, it's not a company that looks after their fans. The most frustrating part is that I know how good they have been and still can be. Shake up that creative process immediately."
    Txndre wrote on 07.05.2026:
    [5.0] "WWE is a great company, and has done a lot overall to make wrestling stand up as a true competitive sport. The real downside to WWE is that the balancing between Entertainment and Wrestling, is most of time really bad. I am still very much fine with giving WWE a positive review for everything they have done, despite all the controversies. Well originally, I was supposed to give a good review, but the Advertising, the budgets cuts to wrestlers and everything that is going with TKO, is outrageous and should not be undermined. Downgraded to original rating I wanted to give."
    AJ Styles was better than your favorite wrote on 04.05.2026:
    [0.0] "WWE started off it's 2026 in a pretty decent way actually. We had a couple of good Raw's, great smackdowns, and a fantastic SNME to top it all off. I don't know what the hell happened but they're currently just as bad as it was in 2025 since the royal rumble happened. Raw has been drizzling shits. The only interesting things on it are CM Punk, Finn Balor, and Je'von Evans. They haven't had Liv decide who she's facing at WrestleMania because even they know they're incapable of putting on a near 3 month build whether it's Liv or Jade. Oh and apparently Stephanie can't speak English so Dominik has to translate everything to her because he totally doesn't hold the ic title or the AAA Mega title. It's bizzare because I've seen Vaquer cut a promo in English and for someone who only moved to the states in late 2024 she's pretty damn good at it. Rhea and Iyo are doing very well with the tag titles and it's one of the few bright spots in the company now, but even they aren't on raw as all their defenses happen on smackdown. When it comes to Becky and the women's ic title it's nothing new. She hasn't done a thing since SummerSlam and the AJ Lee feud has been terrible as I predicted. The iron was hot in September and they should've done the match in October or at war games. Oh, and they're still experimenting with Maxxine only now it's an unwatchable feud with Natalya who hasn't been relevant since 2017. It's unfrotunate because talented wrestlers like Bayley and Lyra are being sacrificed for it. Asuka and Kairi had a very good angle at the rumble and they just haven't followed it up at all. The tag division is non existent which pisses me off because Aj styles spent the final few months of his career rebuilding it. Dragon Lee getting a high profile feud with Gunther is good however. When it comes to smackdown things are a BIT better. Carmelo Hayes has been amazing as champion and has naturally gotten over. Trick has gotten mega over super quickly and it's great to see. Jade Cargill has been having a terrible reign. She defended against jordynne which was her first defence after 104 days. It wasn't even that special and I doubt she's defending it again til WrestleMania. Drew is champion but he still gets booked like a clown which is hard to blame. Cody and Randy would have been a far better program for WrestleMania and if they just gave Drew the title at wrestlepalooza and let him roam around with it for a couple of months they wouldn't have had to worry about giving him a "here damn" reign during WrestleMania season. The tag titles are non existent but that's been the case for months so nothing new. Kit Wilson does pretty entertaining stuff too. The only other good thing is Oba Femi because he should get pushed to the moon this year. Oh, and I didn't even bring up how bad the vision currently is because Seth and Bron's absence has exposed them to a whole new level. Nobody in that group is entertaining and the masked man stuff is about as repetitive as the amount of Raw's they've main evented since WrestleMania 41. Elimination chamber needs to be at least an 8 or a 9 or else WrestleMania is gonna be just as bad as last year. I haven't lost all hope yet but it's not looking good at all right now. Triple H did a fantastic job from the moment he took over up until the fall of 2024. The shows began to get underwhelming, but since the Royal Rumble it's been a butterfly effect of terrible and terrible desicons. Something needs to change, new writers need to be hired. Bruce Prichard, Michael Hayes, Road Dogg, and Ed Koskey are all bad and incredibly out of touch. Younger and more female writters need to be hired. If it still doesn't improve after that it may be time to question Triple H's job at head of creative. He's clearly nowhere as near in the weeds as he used to be and it's not like many of the top writers have any good ideas either. The Bloodline story is done, Cena's retirement tour is done, all these things should clearly indicate that new talent should be getting pushed but I'm not seeing that. They've done a good job with the recent call ups but I'll only praise it once I see what their WrestleMania direction is. Monday Night Raw doesn't even feel like a wrestling show. 2 1/2 hours with only 4 matches on an average basis is hilarious, especially when some of the matches aren't even getting 5 minutes. I feel like I'm seeing the same episode on repeat. They took a step in the right direction in their first show after elimination chamber, now keep doing the same thing and you'll have a good show. It doesn't seem to be happening at all. The fact that Iyo sky and Gunther don't have a proper direction is abysmal. Being the best performer in the company and you're likely facing "Raquel Rodriguez" who's been a failed experiment for almost 6 years now? Make it make sense. It's only getting worse."
    Osla24 wrote on 04.05.2026:
    [2.0] "A corporate sellout. WWE has officially traded its soul for profit. Watching Raw or SmackDown now feels like sitting through a three-hour commercial. Between the digital ads cluttering the ring and the overpriced tickets pricing out real fans, the "magic" is gone. Triple H's booking has become repetitive and risk-averse, dragging out storylines like the Bloodline far past their expiration date. It's a sanitized, corporate product. I'm done with them for a while long now. For me, impossible to watch."
    KhLogan2004 wrote on 03.05.2026:
    [3.0] "WWE has always been a great company, under the McMahon family's ownership. It's had its ups and downs, its rise and fall. Then came Roman Reigns, who were forced upon the fans, and anyone the fans loved, Vince McMahon would humiliate and make a fool of. There were good things in those dark years, though, because no matter how old and senile Vince McMahon became, he still knew how to grab attention and wanted his product to be top-notch, even if it meant making the mistake of not developing stars and relying on wrestlers over 35. Most importantly, his priority was filling the arenas and preventing fans from complaining about ticket prices. He also rarely let a famous star overshadow his wrestlers, except in rare cases like Arnold Schwarzenegger and Mike Tyson. Now, TKO has driven fans away, and ticket prices have skyrocketed. They're milking the system and repeating the same old product without any development. They no longer care about the employees or the wrestlers. It's become normal for them to release injured wrestlers or producers under the pretext of cutting costs, even though they're making millions and they bring in any low-profile online celebrity who's over the top and attacks wrestlers without any problem, and they give him whatever money he wants. I wish the big wrestlers would rebel against them and let the company run rampant as it pleases, and go to someone who actually respects them. That would be the best TKO they've ever had, and they'd lose all their money."
    Paul04 wrote on 02.05.2026:
    [4.0] "They had 2 good years and then decided to become shit once again, is still not as bad as Vince last years but every week keeps getting close, Mania was the biggest example of that, we got a horrible night 1 and pretty good night 2, they have released a lot of great talent and ruined a lot of divisions, specially on SmackDown"
    E69420 wrote on 30.04.2026:
    "The WWE is such a weird company. Despite being a wrestling company, they seem adamant on being an entertainment company like Disney. When they hit the mark, it's one of if not the best product on TV, with great storylines and matches. But when they're in a rut, it can become an unwatchable heap of garbage. The perfect example is the current decade: we started out in a bad spell with stars leaving for AEW and TNA. However, once Triple H took over, it surpassed the competition and proved that "cinematic" sports entertainment was superior to basic professional wrestling. But now, it's a huge mess: you have strong storylines like Roman Reigns vs CM Punk, but also egregious programs that shoehorn in celebrities like Cody Rhodes vs Randy Orton. In short, WWE is a roller coaster of quality that pioneer the industry at their best or make a mockery of the business at its worst"
    wheezpuffs wrote on 28.04.2026:
    [5.0] "Whether you want to admit it or not, this is not the greatest wrestling company to ever exist. It isn't even the best going today. How bout we give this shitty show run by shitty people the shitty flowers they deserve? The wrestlers that have graced this promotion have given the world some of the greatest matches ever produced. Cameramen, video editors, pyro operators, and countless other behind the scenes people have given WWE the highest production quality of any wrestling show for decades. That said, the fatheaded decision makers in charge of creative decisions are dumber than bags of rocks and have been for... ever actually. This company has been around for 60 some odd years now. How can they not figure this stuff out? They've made stars before, albeit reluctantly. These days it honestly seems as though WWE could release its entire roster tomorrow and they would still expect people to pay an arm and a leg to see a live WWE show. We are all wrestling fans here, most of us because of WWE. It really gets me fucking down that this beautiful sport and all these great wrestlers still have to put up with the genuine, unadulterated stupidity at the corporate heart of this entity."
    char1ieone wrote on 28.04.2026:
    [2.0] "For a promotion that has unlimited money and a giant pool of talent to having to nickel and dime every single man, woman and even child is the main reason why I am giving WWE such a low score. If the promotion would provide a sufficient amount of matches to their weekly and even PPV events, the score would be higher, but because of the lack of entertaining the fanbase, attempting every avenue to get any dollar they can at the most limited effort possible and then to insult the fanbase to make claims as to why a certain limited amount of matches are perfect for 3-4 hours shows at the cost they are charging, and that anything that is not WWE involved is garbage or irrelevant to the wrestling landscape is just disgusting. The fact the we only get 3-4 matches for a 3 hour televised event and 4 matches for a 4 hour PPV and even those are mediocre at the very best would rank the promotion to a 5, maybe 6. But then to cram all the ads, sponsorships and attempts to black out fans that cannot afford to attend an event plus the 50% increase to shows from the previous years is what drops this grade further down. The value that WWE may display for shareholders pats them on the shoulder for a job well done, but the value that they are giving to us as an audience would be D-listed in the market if it was based off of value to the consumers."
    RudyPatootie wrote on 27.04.2026:
    [7.0] "WWE is such a strange company, especially for them being the so called "trendsetters" of professional wrestling. They seem to have many peaks and troths, and when they're on it, they are definitely on it, as seen during the Attitude Era, Ruthless Aggression Era and even the slight boom period when Triple H first took charge However, when they're not on the ball, they're nearly unbearable to watch, from bad wrestling to nonsensical storylines and the dreaded celebrity involvement. Remember, just because something is the most popular doesn't mean it will be the best."
    N47H4N H4RDY wrote on 27.04.2026:
    [7.0] "It isn't easy to review an entire promotion. In the grand scheme of things, I've watched so little. I would say the company peaked aesthetically and with its roster in 1996-2004. That feeling was never matched again, despite the company putting out quality matches and rivalries afterwards. The roughest patch was 2010-2022. That's very general and does not apply to (most) of the NXT division, but that's when the company began to get stale and watered down. Once competition had died for a while, they put less effort into the programming, and it shows - the best stuff - the summer of punk, the yes movement - were spun from crowd frustration. Once Vince departed from the creative side, there was a dramatic improvement with all his silly rules gone and a better work environment. It's far from perfect still, the wrong people are getting pushed at the moment, and those with potential have been lowered a little. Still, I continue to follow WWE, especially now, because of its more consistent quality. EDIT: So, since the Main Event of Mania 41, Sunday things have gone downhill, with the blame generally going to TKO, whereas it was originally directed toward Triple H. Whoever is more responsible for the decline in the product is very obvious from a business model standpoint, the main complaints being the cartoonishly expensive tickets that only Mr Burns and Scrooge McDuck can afford, with going broke and the constant advertising. It also breaks my heart that instead of Aleister Black returning to the main event scene, we got Pat McAfee due to out-of-touch billionaires who still think that Dwayne Johnson is on top of the world. Hopefully, their loss of money will convince them to stop tampering with the product."
    Djoxxys wrote on 21.04.2026:
    [10.0] "Whether we like it or not, this is the greatest wrestling company in history, with its ups and downs. Even if, currently (2026), the product they offer isn't amazing, we shouldn't forget everything WWE has given us."
    FearTheResthold wrote on 20.04.2026:
    "Soulless Wrestlemania. No stories, No passion, ads and corporate schlock. This company was my first love and it's a corpse now."
    RanmaLaw wrote on 17.04.2026:
    [2.0] "1 star for any amount of enjoyment that I have had out of it and 1 start for the impact it had on American wrestling through the territory days. But everything else beyond that is imo a personification of everything I hated about Pro wrestling, not only creatively as an artform and how much it clashes with the vision of Vince but also the corporate, monopoly of the medium which was almost always been a goal. Often times questionable work to outright being political currently. Also single handedly responsible for the image of pro wrestling in the way it exists in the culture."
    plsdontclap wrote on 09.04.2026:
    [8.0] "The wrestling in itself is pretty repetitive and choreographed but the WWE is by far the most successful and influential wrestling promotion in history. They are very good at telling stories through backstage segments and promos, and they completely obliterated competition putting an end to the territory system (wether it's a good or a bad thing I let you decide)"
    chaqudemus wrote on 09.04.2026:
    [3.0] "The WWE cares more about entrances, sponsorships, endless promo's and ugly visuals than it does about actual wrestling. It's too bad because they have an absolutely stacked roster across the years. They also pick the most corporate soulless people to lead the company: HHHH, Hogan, Cena, Rhodes... People often say that they have less than stellar wrestling but the stories make it up for that. I don't agree. Just because you repeat the same stuff and show people what happened over and over doesn't mean good storytelling. Lazy and uninspired, risk-averse, boring."
    NorthSideLariat wrote on 09.04.2026:
    [9.0] "WWE remains the cornerstone of professional wrestling and the reason the industry is where it is today, with an unmatched legacy of stars across every era."
    MrAicrow wrote on 06.04.2026:
    [9.0] "WWE is the reason wrestling became mainstream, Without WWE, We wouldn't have this website and a giant community, WWE has so many legends, Sammartino in the 70s, Hogan, Savage and Piper in the 80s, Hart, Michaels, Rock and Austin in the 90s, Undertaker, Triple H and Batista in the 2000s, Cena, Orton and Bryan in the 2010s and now Reigns, Rollins and Rhodes now adays However, Modern WWE is not like the old WWF, Now all they care about is money, The ticket prices, Sponsorships on the sacred ring, The multiple subscriptions just to watch, It sucks, TKO has somewhat ruined the WWE, I will still rate it a 10 because this is the reason we are where we are EDIT (April 6th, 2026): It's really gotten worse, It's awful now, I am gonna have to knock it down by 1. It's like everyone's brains shutdown before writing the road to Wrestlemania."
    MooGati wrote on 05.04.2026:
    [1.0] "Beyond the creative bankruptcy and appeasement to sponsors and shareholders over fans, there's the decades of abuse that Vince and co put others through. Vince McMahon may only be facing consequences for his abusive behaviour now, but you'd have to be deaf dumb and blind to not hear the stories that have been told about him and his ilk for decades as 'good ol boy' stories that are really just more and more stories of a rich insulated creep who could treat anyone how he wanted. The one point WWE gets from me is taht Vince no longer works there, but that's about it in the positive column for me."
    xterminator519 wrote on 05.04.2026:
    "Just another terrible SmackDown episode, like I'm used to it at this point. It almost feels intentional. Like they hold back on doing anything great weekly so that the big moments hit harder, but right now it just makes the shows feel flat. I think Pat has potential as a heel, but inserting him into this storyline just hurt him more than anything. Heel Randy Orton doesn't need help, and if he did, it should be someone with real presence or authority, not this. And then there's Sami and Carmelo. They're clearly using Carmelo as a stepping stone to turn Sami heel, and in the process, they're killing all of Carmelo's momentum. It's frustrating to see someone with so much potential being used like that just to serve another storyline. At this point, I'll probably just stick to YouTube highlights, until even that stops being worth it."
    Dempsey956 wrote on 02.04.2026:
    "The company that started my pro-wrestling fandom from 2003-onward! It started on an episode of Monday Night RAW on 3/3/03."
    SnowBunnyAlert45 wrote on 02.04.2026:
    [6.0] "Theres a reason why this company is still alive, and thats because Vince McMahon broke the gentlemen's agreement, and monopolized pro wrestling in the US. To be completely honest, I enjoyed the WWE product for a while, and that's because I was 9, and thought that WWE was the only pro-wrestling show out there, which goes to show how much WWE have monopolized the pro wrestling market. To be even more honest, the WWE product can sometimes be really good, but in hindsight, the few good things were having a show that essentially was indie-wrestling, but easily accessable, and turning 1 (one) wrestler heel, and running with it for 4 fucking years. Theres a reason why AEW ratings are going up, and thats because Cody "Hogan" Rhodes won't stop holding on to a championship that he didn't deserve. If you were to ask me to rate just NXT, I'd give it way higher than WWE."
    kj wrote on 30.03.2026:
    [3.0] "The past few years, WWE has been awful. They constantly put on bad shows, yet ticket prices are crazy high. They focus more on everything that is not wrestling, and the matches rarely deliver. Bringing in celebrities to try and build hype is not making it any better, and it takes away from actual hardworking talent. Storylines feel repetitive and drag on too long, which kills my interest to keep watching. I used to enjoy watching this company, but that is not the case anymore."
    rjstanford wrote on 30.03.2026:
    [0.0] "[Triple H adjusts his tie, sits down at a desk in front of a monitor showing WWE Raw. He holds up a laminated printout of the TV schedule.] "Paul here. You may have heard about it. Here it is. WWE Raw." [He taps the printout gently.] "I love this product. It is so good. This is something we have already tested -- in developmental, in NXT, and in the United Kingdom. We've tested it on the British and the developmental talent, and now we believe it may even be fit for human consumption." [He sets the printout down and turns to face the screen. A six-man tag match is happening. He stares at it with the energy of a man who has never watched professional wrestling before.] "Holy cow. God, that is a big match. We've got... sort of the athleticism and the grappling. The storytelling is in there. And of course... we've got the finishes. I'm going to watch the rest of this for my Monday evening, just so you know." [He leans forward, picks up the remote, and takes a very small, hesitant click of the volume button. He holds the remote up to the camera to prove it happened.] "That is a big click for a big show. It's distinctively WWE. Only WWE could do this type of product. But it is also unlike anything else on our schedule." [He gently sets the remote down and smiles at the camera.] "It's sort of got promos and emotion. There's so much going on with this." [Long pause.] "I'm going to enjoy the rest of my Monday, but -- WWE Raw -- watch it when you can. On Netflix." [He nods once, professionally, as if he has just concluded a board presentation. The match continues behind him. A steel chair is introduced. He does not react.]"
    Macca666 wrote on 28.03.2026:
    [1.0] "If I was judging the company on the late 90's and the early 2000's, then I would be giving them a 9, but let's face it, the product hasn't been good for some years now. I was so disappointed with them during covid when they practically discarded a large amount of the roster just to save money, some of who had been with them for 10+ years. Then with Vince gone, you would think it would get better, but it has gotten worse. They completely screwed everyone over in Europe, by turning the WWE network off and moving the content to Netflix, where you only have access to around 20% of the content that we had before. Let's talk about the content these days, on Raw we only get 4 matches in 2 hours each week, but they're happy for Jey Uso to spend 20 mins walking to the ring and dance like an idiot. Smackdown is just a snooze fest. Then there's the PLE's, 3 hours for 4 to 5 matches is a joke, it wouldn't be so bad if the matches were good, but they are often dog shit. I feel sorry for the fans in the USA who have to pay around $1, 000 a year to watch this shit."
    0xy3buu9w wrote on 27.03.2026:
    [0.0] "Nothing is entertaining at this company. Yes they have the lights and the big crowds but that's it. Video packages are lazy and made with AI despite WWE generating $1.7b in revenue in 2025. Entrance themes are the most generic they've ever been making no wrestler outside of the top handful of guys feel like they're worth following. Matches are far and few in between weekly shows and even more so when it comes to main event guys and when matches do happen, they are yawn inducing overscripted throwaways, wrestlers are reduced to catchphrases and have little to no character development, ads in and out of the ring are overwhelming and meme worthy, constant pointless "celebrity" appearances and matches, constant long winded backstage segments, constant "legends" returning that should've stayed away, horrible promos and acting with long "feuds" for the sake of "long term storytelling" involving said "legends", and to top it all of, the thing you hear all the time, cringe worthy commentary that makes you want to never watch another WWE show again. Save your time and go watch a real sports entertainment company called Actwres girl'Z. Superhuman Asako Army > WWE"
    1mths wrote on 27.03.2026:
    "WWE is currently in one of its strongest and most consistent phases in years, earning a realistic rating of 8.2 out of 10. Since the shift in creative direction, the company has improved significantly in long-term storytelling, with narratives that actually develop over time instead of being dropped randomly. However, it still struggles with consistency across the entire card, as some storylines lose momentum and the midcard can feel underdeveloped. When it comes to promos and character work, WWE remains one of the best in the world. Top stars are presented extremely well and feel like true main eventers, but not every talent is given material that allows them to shine naturally, which can make some performances feel scripted or restricted. In-ring quality has also improved a lot, with a stronger focus on storytelling and physicality, especially during premium live events, although weekly matches can still be predictable and less impactful compared to other companies that emphasize pure wrestling. Production value is where WWE clearly stands above everyone else, delivering a polished, almost cinematic presentation that enhances the overall experience, even if it can sometimes feel overly produced. Perhaps its greatest strength is star creation, as WWE still understands how to build and present major names better than anyone, even if it occasionally takes too long to fully commit to certain talents. Overall, WWE is no longer at its lowest point, but it is not flawless either; it is a dominant company that has regained stability and direction, yet still has room to grow before reaching true perfection."
    Read all comments
